lessons.title: Chemical Equilibrium and Le Chatelier's Principle

content.body: Many chemical reactions are reversible — they can proceed in both directions. At equilibrium, the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal, so concentrations remain constant (but not zero).

Equilibrium constant Kc:
aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD
Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b

Kc >> 1: equilibrium lies to right (products favoured)
Kc << 1: equilibrium lies to left (reactants favoured)

Note: pure solids and liquids not included in Kc expression
Kp: equilibrium constant using partial pressures (for gases)

content.title: Le Chatelier's Principle
content.body:
If an equilibrium system is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change:

+ reactant concentration: shifts RIGHT (more product)
+ product concentration: shifts LEFT
+ pressure (gas): shifts towards fewer moles of gas
+ temperature: if exothermic reaction, shifts LEFT (oppose heat);
  if endothermic reaction, shifts RIGHT
Catalyst: speeds up both forward and reverse equally — no shift in equilibrium position, just faster attainment

content.title: Haber Process
content.body:
N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃  ΔH = -92 kJ/mol
Conditions: 450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst

Le Chatelier analysis:
• High pressure favours NH₃ (4 mol → 2 mol)
• Low temperature favours NH₃ (exothermic) — but slow rate
• Compromise: 450°C (reasonable rate + yield), 200 atm
• Fe catalyst reaches equilibrium faster, not more NH₃

questions.question: At chemical equilibrium
options: rate of forward reaction equals rate of reverse reaction
questions.answer: rate of forward reaction equals rate of reverse reaction
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: dynamic state where both reactions continue at equal rates, so concentrations remain constant.

questions.question: Kc >> 1 means
options: equilibrium lies to the right, products are favoured
questions.answer: equilibrium lies to the right, products are favoured
questions.explanation: Large Kc means products are much more concentrated than reactants at equilibrium.

questions.question: Le Chatelier's principle states
options: a disturbed equilibrium shifts to oppose the change and restore equilibrium
questions.answer: a disturbed equilibrium shifts to oppose the change and restore equilibrium
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier: if equilibrium is disturbed, system responds by shifting direction to minimise the disturbance.

questions.question: For N₂+3H₂⇌2NH₃, increasing pressure
options: shifts right (4 mol→2 mol, fewer moles of gas)
questions.answer: shifts right (4 mol→2 mol, fewer moles of gas)
questions.explanation: High pressure favours the side with fewer moles of gas: 4 mol → 2 mol, so shifts right, producing more NH₃.

questions.question: For an exothermic reaction, increasing temperature
options: decreases yield of products (shifts left, opposing heat)
questions.answer: decreases yield of products (shifts left, opposing heat)
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature adds heat energy. Exothermic reaction produces heat, so system shifts LEFT (endothermic direction) to oppose.

questions.question: A catalyst's effect on equilibrium is to
options: have no effect on equilibrium position — only speeds both reactions equally
questions.answer: have no effect on equilibrium position — only speeds both reactions equally
questions.explanation: Catalysts reduce activation energy equally for forward and reverse reactions — equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.

questions.question: The Haber process uses 450°C (not lower, which would give more NH₃) because
options: lower temperature gives an impractically slow reaction rate
questions.answer: lower temperature gives an impractically slow reaction rate
questions.explanation: Compromise: at low T, yield is high but rate too slow for industrial use. 450°C balances acceptable yield with practical rate.

questions.question: For 2SO₂+O₂⇌2SO₃, Kc expression is
questions.explanation: Kc = [products]^n / [reactants]^n using stoichiometric coefficients as powers.

questions.question: Adding more reactant to an equilibrium system
options: shifts equilibrium to the right, producing more product
questions.answer: shifts equilibrium to the right, producing more product
questions.explanation: Adding reactant increases reactant concentration. System shifts right to partially oppose this by consuming some added reactant.

questions.question: Removing product from an equilibrium system
questions.explanation: Removing product decreases product concentration. System shifts right to replace it, producing more product — useful industrially.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Brønsted-Lowry definitions:
Acid: proton (H⁺) donor
Base: proton (H⁺) acceptor

Strong acids fully dissociate: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻
Weak acids partially dissociate: CH₃COOH ⇌ H⁺ + CH₃COO⁻

pH = -log[H⁺]
[H⁺] = 10^(-pH)
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10^(-14) at 25°C
pH + pOH = 14

Acid dissociation constant:
Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]
pKa = -log Ka

content.title: pH Calculations
content.body:
Strong acid: 0.01 M HCl
[H⁺] = 0.01 mol/dm³
pH = -log(0.01) = 2

Strong base: 0.001 M NaOH
[OH⁻] = 0.001
pOH = 3
pH = 14 - 3 = 11

Weak acid: 0.1M CH₃COOH, Ka=1.8×10⁻⁵
[H⁺] = √(Ka×c) = √(1.8×10⁻⁶) = 1.34×10⁻³
pH = 2.87

content.title: Buffer Solutions
content.body:
Buffer: resists change in pH when small amounts of acid/base added
Made from: weak acid + its conjugate base (salt)
e.g. CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa

Add acid (H⁺): H⁺ + CH₃COO⁻ → CH₃COOH (absorbed)
Add alkali (OH⁻): OH⁻ + CH₃COOH → CH₃COO⁻ + H₂O (absorbed)
Blood buffer: H₂CO₃/HCO₃⁻ maintains blood pH ~7.4

questions.question: A Brønsted-Lowry acid is
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry definition: acid = H⁺ (proton) donor; base = H⁺ (proton) acceptor.

questions.question: pH of 0.1 mol/dm³ HCl
questions.explanation: Strong acid fully dissociates: [H⁺]=0.1M. pH=-log(0.1)=1

questions.question: [H⁺] when pH=3
pH of NaOH solution with [OH⁻]=0.01 mol/dm³
Weak acid has lower pH than strong acid at same concentration because
questions.explanation: Weak acid only partially ionises → lower [H⁺] → higher pH than strong acid of same concentration.

questions.question: Ka is large for
options: strong acids (large Ka means near complete dissociation)
questions.answer: strong acids (large Ka means near complete dissociation)
questions.explanation: Large Ka: equilibrium lies far to the right → acid mostly dissociated → strong acid. Small Ka = weak acid.

questions.question: A buffer solution resists pH change by
options: containing a weak acid/base pair that neutralises added acid or base
questions.answer: containing a weak acid/base pair that neutralises added acid or base
questions.explanation: Buffer's conjugate acid/base pair can absorb either added H⁺ or OH⁻, preventing significant pH change.

questions.question: Blood pH is maintained at ~7.4 by the
questions.explanation: The bicarbonate buffer (carbonic acid/bicarbonate) is the main blood pH regulator, supplemented by haemoglobin and phosphate buffers.

questions.question: pKa = -log Ka. A smaller pKa means
questions.explanation: Smaller pKa → larger Ka → more dissociation → stronger acid. pKa and Ka have inverse relationship.

questions.question: Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C. In pure water, [H⁺] =
questions.explanation: In pure water [H⁺]=[OH⁻], so [H⁺]²=10⁻¹⁴, [H⁺]=10⁻⁷, giving pH=7 (neutral).

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Equilibrium. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Equilibrium concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Equilibrium builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Equilibrium. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Equilibrium. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Equilibrium include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Equilibrium. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Equilibrium. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Equilibrium concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Equilibrium builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Equilibrium. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Equilibrium. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Equilibrium include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Equilibrium. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
